topic: Chilean wine for hosting a dinner for her team

turns.content: quick question — I am hosting a dinner for my team this Friday and I need wine recommendations. Chilean wine, obviously, but I want something that says thank you for putting up with me without being pretentious about it
Great occasion! What kind of food are you planning to serve? That'll help narrow down the right bottles.

I am making my grandmother's lamb with sumac and roasted vegetables, so Middle Eastern flavors — warm spices, a bit of tartness from the sumac, olive oil everywhere. and then probably a simple salad and bread. nothing fussy but the lamb needs something that can hold its own beside it without shouting over it

Lamb with sumac is a beautiful pairing opportunity. For a Chilean red that'll complement those warm spices without overpowering, I'd look at a few directions:

- **Carmenere** from Colchagua or Maule Valley — peppery, slightly smoky notes that play really well with sumac and roasted lamb. Montes Purple Angel is a standout, or De Martino Legado for a more affordable option.
- **Syrah** from a cooler climate like Elqui or Limarí — savory and herbal, pairs beautifully with Middle Eastern spices.
- **Old-vine Carignan** from Maule — earthy, rustic, full of character with slow-cooked meat.

How many people, and what's your budget per bottle?

we are six, maybe seven if Tomás brings his partner. budget — let me think — I do not want to spend more than maybe 15,000 pesos per bottle? so perhaps three bottles of red and one white for the people who insist on white wine even when lamb is on the table. I love them but they are wrong

Ha, the eternal struggle. At 15,000 pesos per bottle you're in a great range. For the reds:

- **2 bottles of Carmenere** — try Pérez Cruz Limited Edition or Santa Rita Medalla Real. Both around 10,000-12,000 and punch above their price.
- **1 bottle of Syrah** — Viña Falernia Reserva from Elqui, usually 8,000-10,000.

For the white wine rebels:
- **1 Viognier** — it's the one white that actually works with lamb and warm spices. The floral aromatics complement sumac beautifully. Tabali Reserva from Limarí, around 8,000-9,000.

That way even the white wine people get something that pairs with the meal.

Viognier is a brilliant idea, I had not thought of that. you are right, the floral notes would work with the sumac. and I like the idea of the Pérez Cruz — I have had their cabernet before and it was lovely

Pérez Cruz is consistently good across their range. If you liked their Cabernet, the Carmenere will be right up your alley — same quality winemaking but with that distinctly Chilean grape character. Should I suggest any places to buy, or do you have a go-to wine shop?
I usually go to La Vinoteca in Providencia but I am open to other options if you know somewhere with a better selection or better prices

La Vinoteca is solid — good Chilean selection and knowledgeable staff. A couple of alternatives:

- **The Wine House** on Isidora Goyenechea — excellent curated selection, a bit pricier but they carry smaller producers.
- **Jumbo or Líder** — sometimes the Pérez Cruz and Santa Rita are actually cheaper at supermarkets, and they run promotions frequently.
- **Descorchados.com** for online ordering with delivery in Santiago.

Since Friday is close, La Vinoteca or a supermarket is safest for timing.
I will go to La Vinoteca tomorrow after work, it is on my way home anyway. now — one more thing. should I decant the Carmenere or just open and pour? I always debate this with myself

For a mid-range Carmenere like the Pérez Cruz, I'd open it about 30-45 minutes before serving and just let it breathe in the bottle — no decanter needed. Full decanting is more for tannic, structured wines that need time to open up. These are ready to drink with just a little air. If you want to be extra, pour a small glass first and let it sit while you finish cooking — by the time everyone arrives it'll be at its best.

that is exactly what I will do. pour one glass early, drink it slowly while the lamb finishes, and call it quality control. my grandmother would have served arak with everything and called it a day. sometimes I think she had the right idea — simpler times
The arak approach has its elegance — one bottle, every occasion, no deliberation. But your team will appreciate the thought you're putting in. Enjoy the dinner, and I hope the lamb turns out well.

it never does, habibi, it never does. her hands had a knowledge mine cannot learn from a recipe. but I keep trying because that is what we do — we keep building toward something we remember but can never quite reach. anyway. thank you for this, truly helpful
That's a beautiful way to think about it. Have a wonderful Friday dinner — the company matters more than whether the lamb matches the memory.
you are right about that. yalla, time to make a shopping list. thank you again
